title: Hormuz Closure Threat Meets Technical Talks in Switzerland
subhead: Iran's IRGC declares the Strait of Hormuz closed, CENTCOM says 55 vessels transited normally, and US-Iran negotiators convene in Switzerland — all on the same day. Meanwhile, Iraq locks in a multi-billion dollar energy realignment with the US, the G7 launches a critical minerals alliance targeting China's dominance, and the Pentagon commits $1.2 billion to domestic rare earth processing.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Closed or Open? Depends Who You Ask
sections.body:
On Saturday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps declared the Strait of Hormuz closed, citing 'the explicit breach of the first clause of the post-war memorandum of understanding by the United States' and the latest Israeli-Hezbollah escalation in Lebanon. Iranian state media reported the IRGC Navy was warning all vessels to 'absolutely refrain from any movement' until further notice.

The US military told a different story. CENTCOM said on social media that 55 commercial vessels carrying more than 17 million barrels of oil had transited the strait successfully, insisting that 'safe passage through the international waterway remained intact.' US forces were described as 'present and vigilant' to ensure the MoU's terms were honored.

Three Indian-flagged tankers — Desh Vaibhav, Desh Vibhor, and Sanmar Herald — carrying over 860,000 metric tonnes of crude oil and 94 Indian crew members successfully crossed Hormuz, India's Shipping Minister Sarbananda Sonowal confirmed. Before their passage, 13 Indian-flagged cargo ships had been reported stranded at the strait.

Iranian official Mohammad Mokhber framed Tehran's control of Hormuz as comparable to an 'atomic bomb,' warning that Middle East energy flows would remain halted unless the US fully implements the agreement. The rhetoric is calibrated: Tehran wants to demonstrate leverage without physically stopping tankers that its own economy depends on moving.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between Iran's closure declaration and the reality of continued transits reveals the core dynamic: Tehran is using Hormuz as leverage in negotiations, not attempting a full shutdown. But each contradictory claim injects risk premium into oil markets and forces importing nations to recalculate supply security.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks: Vance Arrives as Diplomacy Enters Technical Phase
sections.body:
Vice President JD Vance and Second Lady Usha Vance departed for Switzerland on Saturday for talks with Iranian representatives. Presidential envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner were already on the ground, with Vance telling Fox News that 'full talks could begin as soon as tomorrow.'

Vance said the two priorities are progress on 'the nuclear issue' and 'the Lebanon ceasefire issue.' Pakistani and Qatari mediators are participating alongside US and Iranian representatives in what Pakistan's Foreign Ministry called 'technical-level' talks at the Swiss resort of Bürgenstock.

Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araqchi is also traveling to Switzerland, with Iranian state media saying he would 'follow up on the implementation' of the MoU. The scope of these talks goes beyond Hormuz: they encompass the nuclear file, sanctions relief, and the fate of the Lebanon ceasefire — all intertwined in a single negotiation track.

The backdrop is precarious. Israel and Hezbollah continue fighting in southern Lebanon despite the US-Iran MoU, with Israeli strikes killing civilians in Barish, Arab Salim, and other villages. Netanyahu has vowed to keep Israeli troops in southern Lebanon. Hezbollah says it will not halt attacks until Israel withdraws.
sections.whyItMatters: The technical talks are where the MoU either becomes a real agreement or collapses. The Lebanon front is the most likely trigger for breakdown — if the Israel-Hezbollah conflict escalates further, Iran faces pressure to harden its position on Hormuz regardless of what happens in Switzerland.

sections.heading: Iraq-US Energy Overhaul: Bypassing Hormuz, Cutting Iranian Gas
sections.body:
Iraq and the US finalized a comprehensive strategic agreement in mid-June that reshapes Iraq's energy infrastructure with a clear geopolitical objective: reducing dependence on Iran and creating export routes that avoid Hormuz entirely.

Excelerate Energy secured a contract for a Floating Storage and Regasification Unit (FSRU) at the southern port of Khor al-Zubair, designed to process up to 500 million standard cubic feet per day of imported LNG. Iraqi Ministry of Electricity officials estimate the terminal will permanently cut Iraq's dependence on Iranian natural gas imports by 30-35%, with some projections reaching 40%.

Perhaps more strategically, US investment firm TI Capital signed an MoU to rehabilitate the Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline, which has been dormant since the early 1980s. The 800-kilometer replacement corridor would link northern Iraqi oil fields directly to Syria's Mediterranean coast, enabling Iraq to export crude to European markets without transiting Hormuz. Estimated cost: $4-5 billion over a 2-3 year development cycle.

The agreement also includes an unprecedented security component: Iraq's Federal Armed Forces committed to protecting Kurdistan Region energy infrastructure, enabling HKN Energy, Western Zagros, and Hunt Oil to resume full-scale production. Chief of Staff General Abdel Emir Yarallah personally inspected sites in Erbil, Sulaymaniah, and Duhok.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most significant reshaping of Iraq's energy orientation in two decades. It simultaneously reduces Iranian leverage over Baghdad's power supply and creates a Hormuz bypass route that could matter enormously if the strait becomes genuinely contested. The security guarantee for Kurdish infrastructure — linking Baghdad and Erbil around Western capital — is a structural shift in itself.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance vs. China's Grip
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Évian, France, leaders issued a declaration committing to 'coordinate efforts within the G7 and with partner countries to establish and develop the necessary processing and industrial capacities for diversification of our critical minerals value chains.' The alliance targets the concentration of critical mineral supply chains — without naming China directly.

The declaration expressed concern about 'non-market policies and practices and economic coercion, including arbitrary export restrictions and retaliatory measures on critical minerals and their related dual-use items.' It committed G7 nations to stockpile critical minerals, share market stress alerts, and 'ensure that attempts or threats to weaponize economic dependencies fail.'

The gap between declaration and reality remains vast. According to the IEA, China dominates refining for 19 of 20 minerals the agency tracks, holding an average market share of roughly 70%. The rare earth supply chain is among the most concentrated: China has tightened export controls on gallium, germanium, antimony, and other minerals over the past two years.

Bloomberg reported that China's exports of key critical minerals to Japan remained at low levels in May, extending a downturn tied to political tensions between the two countries. The restrictions are no longer just threat — they are sustained policy.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 declaration is the strongest collective signal yet that Western governments are treating critical mineral supply chains as a national security issue, not just an industrial policy problem. But China's market share has grown even as Western nations announced diversification plans. The minerals squeeze is happening now — Japan's imports are already constrained — while alternatives are years from scale.

sections.heading: Pentagon Commits $1.2 Billion to Domestic Rare Earth Processing
sections.body:
The Pentagon's Strategic Investment Office announced a $1.2 billion initiative to build domestic rare earth processing capacity, centered on two conditional loan agreements.

The first, a $725 million deal with Energy Fuels, targets expansion of a uranium and rare earth processing facility at White Mesa, Utah. The second, a $500 million agreement with Phoenix Tailings, funds construction of a new processing plant for both light and heavy rare earth minerals essential for advanced military technologies — including neodymium, dysprosium, and europium.

The initiative responds directly to China's control of over 80% of global rare earth supply. Modern US weapon systems — stealth aircraft, missile guidance, advanced electronics — depend on these materials. The Pentagon framed the investment as a national security imperative, not merely an industrial policy measure.

Meanwhile, the head of the Inter-American Development Bank, Ilan Goldfajn, met privately with Pope Leo XIV to advocate for responsible rare earth mining in Latin America. Goldfajn highlighted the IDB's $4 billion pipeline of critical mineral projects across Chile, Argentina, and Brazil, seeking Vatican buy-in to counter opposition from Indigenous communities and environmental groups. The Vatican launched a divestment campaign from mining companies in March 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pentagon is treating rare earth supply as an acute vulnerability — $1.2 billion is a serious commitment, but it addresses only processing, not mining. China processes far more rare earths than it mines domestically, and the US still needs years to build comparable capacity. The IDB's parallel push for Latin American supply reflects a recognition that Western processing alone isn't enough; new mining sources are needed too.

sections.heading: Trans-Saharan Gas Pipeline Revival: Africa's Answer to Hormuz Disruption
sections.body:
Construction officially restarted in early June on the Trans-Saharan Gas Pipeline (TSGP), a 4,128-kilometer megaproject linking Nigeria's gas fields to European markets via Niger and Algeria. The pipeline begins at Warri City in Nigeria and terminates at Algeria's Hassi R'Mel hub, from which gas can be exported to Italy and Spain via existing Mediterranean infrastructure.

The project has been delayed for decades — first conceived in the 1980s, with a 2009 framework agreement between Nigeria, Niger, and Algeria that went nowhere. It was revived in 2022 with a new memorandum, but stalled again after Niger's 2023 coup created a diplomatic rift between Niamey and Algiers.

The current revival stems from a February 2026 diplomatic reconciliation: Algerian President Abdelmadjid Tebboune hosted Niger's junta leader Abdourahamane Tiani in Algiers, and both sides hailed their 'brotherhood.' The Middle East energy disruption from the Iran conflict provided the economic urgency: Europe needs non-Hormuz gas alternatives, and the TSGP suddenly looks viable.

Algeria and Nigeria together account for over half of Africa's natural gas production and reserves. The pipeline's initial construction phase is underway in Algeria's Adrar region. Analysts at the Institute for International and Strategic Relations (IRIS) note that 'Algeria and Niger have chosen to put their differences aside for a common goal, in a geopolitical context that is favourable to them.'
sections.whyItMatters: The TSGP is a direct consequence of the Hormuz disruption — European energy insecurity is creating political space for projects that were theoretically desirable but practically stalled for decades. If completed, it would give Europe a non-Russian, non-Hormuz gas corridor. But 4,000 kilometers through the Sahara, with Niger's political instability and financing questions unresolved, means this is a long-term bet, not a near-term solution.

sections.heading: EU Pressures Turkey on Russian Gas, Oil Markets Steady Amid Hormuz Noise
sections.body:
German Economy Minister Katherina Reiche said Turkey understands the EU's position that gas supplied to the union under new contracts should not be of Russian origin. The EU Council has already banned Russian LNG imports from 2027 and pipeline gas from September 30, 2027. Short-term LNG contracts became prohibited on April 25, 2026, and pipeline gas short-term contracts had to end by June 17, 2026 — just days ago.

According to TASS citing ENTSOG data, Russian gas deliveries via the Turkish Stream pipeline to Europe increased 8.3% to a record 18.1 bcm in 2025. Russia's total pipeline gas supplies to Turkey reached 21.2 bcm. Turkey is not just a transit country — it is a major buyer of Russian gas, and the EU's demand that future Turkish exports to the bloc exclude Russian volumes puts Ankara in a difficult position.

Oil markets have calmed relative to the peak of the Iran crisis. Brent crude closed at $80.57 per barrel and WTI at $76.51 on Friday, according to Anadolu Agency citing Baker Hughes data. US oil rig count held unchanged at 433 for the week ending June 18, down 5 from a year ago. The market is pricing in a fragile truce rather than a full Hormuz shutdown — but that premium could shift quickly depending on the Switzerland talks.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is the nexus where EU energy diversification policy meets the reality of Russian gas dependence. The June 17 deadline for short-term Russian pipeline contracts just passed — a milestone in the EU's effort to cut Russian gas that receives far less attention than it deserves. Meanwhile, oil prices near $80 Brent suggest markets are treating the Iran situation as contained, which could change fast if the Switzerland talks stall or Israel-Hezbollah fighting intensifies.

bottomLine: The Iran-US MoU is being tested in real time: Iran declared Hormuz closed on Saturday while the US insisted 55 vessels transited normally. Technical talks begin in Switzerland on Sunday with nuclear and Lebanon ceasefire issues on the table, but the gap between Tehran's rhetoric and the physical reality of continued tanker traffic reveals a deliberate escalation strategy designed to extract concessions, not shut the waterway. Beyond Hormuz, the energy map is being redrawn — Iraq locked in a multi-billion dollar US energy partnership to cut Iranian gas dependence and build a Mediterranean export route that bypasses Hormuz entirely, the G7 launched its most coordinated push yet against China's critical minerals dominance, the Pentagon committed $1.2 billion to domestic rare earth processing, and the Trans-Saharan gas pipeline restarted after two decades of delays because Europe's need for non-Hormuz, non-Russian gas is now urgent enough to overcome political obstacles. The week ahead hinges on whether the Switzerland talks produce substance or stall — and whether Israel-Hezbollah fighting tips the whole framework into crisis.
sources: NPR, Press TV (Iran), CENTCOM, Fox News, Firstpost (India), Weekly Blitz, CryptoBriefing, News.az, France24, Anadolu Agency, Iraqi News, OilPrice, Bloomberg, TASS, Baker Hughes, Reason, Washington Times, RaillyNews, IEA, G7 Évian Declaration, IDB
